front: Battle: began when Russian ships sailed from Crimea and attacked the Ottomans, demonstrating the effectiveness of explosive shells
Event: saw the first-ever battle between two steamships, resulting in the capture of the Egyptian ship Pervas-i Bahri
Event: British preparations for this war were made beforehand by Sir James Graham and the Duke of Newcastle
Event: ignited by a Catholic-Orthodox monk's quarrel over access to the Holy Land
Battle: British commander Lord Raglan and French commander Francois Canrobert fought Russian commander Vladimir Kornilov during this siege
Event: Russian bureaucrats' theft of money intended for ammunition contributed to Russia's loss at this siege
Battle: the 93rd Highlanders under Colin Campbell formed a thin double-rank line, earning the nickname the "Thin Red Line," to repulse a Russian cavalry charge
Event: a miscommunicated order from Lord Raglan to Lord Cardigan caused a doomed British cavalry assault, later known as this
back: Charge of the Light Brigade (Battle of Balaclava)

front: Battle: fought in dense fog, which both allowed Russian reinforcements to arrive and prevented organized unit fighting, earning it the nickname the "Soldier's Battle"
Battle: often regarded as the war's turning point, fought for control of the naval base at Sevastopol
Event: the failure of Russia's peasant armies at battles like Inkerman prompted the creation of a new local-government institution after the war
Concept: this nurse developed a "polar area diagram"/wedge chart, resembling a bat's wing, to track monthly causes of death, assisted by Selina Bracebridge
Figure: this war office inspector's diaries reveal disdain for Florence Nightingale, leading him to support the rival nurse Mary Francis Bridgeman
Figure: this Jamaican-born nurse independently set up the "British Hotel" to care for soldiers

Figure: this Russian physician developed a battlefield triage system and a namesake foot amputation technique that preserves the heel; the "Exaltation of the Holy Cross Community" nurses served under him
Work: this prefabricated wood hospital building was designed by Isambard Kingdom Brunel
Figure: this celebrity chef designed a "magic stove" for soldiers, which was documented by journalist William Howard Russell
Work: this Roger Fenton photograph, taken during the Siege of Sevastopol, depicts cannonballs lying scattered on a deserted road
back: The Valley of the Shadow of Death (Roger Fenton)

front: Artist: this artist's watercolors of the war were commissioned by the firm Colnaghi and Co.
Work: this Robert Gibb painting memorialized soldiers being told "there is no retreat from here, men; you must die where you stand"
Event: bells taken as war spoils from the Church of the Twelve Apostles were later installed in this English royal residence's Round Tower
Event: the newspaper Atlas spread rumors of a fictitious weapon, the "Boulet Asphyxiant," later dispelled by rumors of a countermeasure called MacIntosh's Portable Buoyant Wave Repressor
Work: this three-part story collection comprises "December," "May," and "August"; "May" features Captain Mikhailov capturing a trench
Work: this poem describes "the six hundred" riding "into the valley of Death" during the Charge of the Light Brigade
back: The Charge of the Light Brigade (Alfred, Lord Tennyson)

front: Work: this Rudyard Kipling poem, written in response to Tennyson's Light Brigade poem, describes soldiers who "talked of England's might" yet "were only shiftless soldiers"
back: The Last of the Light Brigade (Rudyard Kipling)

front: Work: this George Meredith novel's protagonist, Nevil Beauchamp, serves in the Crimean War
Work: this James Joyce novel includes a radio program, hosted by Butt and Taff, in which a character named Buckley shoots a general during the Crimean War
Event: this war's final action was fought at the town of Kars, where William Fenwick Williams's forces fought Muravyov-Karsky
Event: these two Danubian principalities were united in the war's aftermath
Event: a declaration that the losing side "was not sulking but meditating" gave its name to this period of reflection, during which glasnost took on its modern political meaning
Event: cold weather forced the British army to relax its facial-hair restrictions during this war, and the triumphant return of bearded soldiers set a new fashion trend
